questions.question:
A queue Q containing n items and an empty stack S are given. It is required to transfer all the items from the queue to the stack, so that the item at the front of queue is on the TOP of the stack, and the order of all other items are preserved. Show how this can be done in O(n) time using only a constant amount of additional storage. Note that the only operations which can be performed on the queue and stack are Delete, Insert, Push and Pop. Do not assume any implementation of the queue or stack.

What is the minimum number of operations required?
questions.to_find: How to transfer n items from a queue to a stack in O(n) time so that front of queue is on top of stack

understand.plain: We have a queue with n items and an empty stack. We need to move all items so that whatever was at the front of the queue is now at the top of the stack. The tricky part is that a queue gives items in FIFO order and a stack operates in LIFO order. We need to reverse the order when transferring. The key insight is: dequeue all items from Q into S (this reverses the order), then we are done since the front element ends up on top.

keywords.explain: A data structure where items are inserted at the rear and deleted from the front — First In, First Out.
A data structure where items are inserted and removed from the top — Last In, First Out.
We may only use O(1) extra memory — no extra array or another queue/stack.
keywords.example: A single temporary variable to hold the dequeued item.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 understand.visual_alt: Queue with items 1..n (front=1), and after transferring, Stack with item 1 on top and n at bottom.

given.aim: Transfer all n items from queue Q to stack S such that the front of Q ends up on top of S, in O(n) time with O(1) extra space.

terms.meaning: Number of items in the queue Q.
terms.connects: Determines how many Delete+Push operations are needed.

terms.meaning: Remove and return the item at the front of the queue.
terms.connects: Used to extract each item from Q one at a time.

terms.meaning: Add an item to the top of the stack.
terms.example: Push(S,1) then Push(S,2): top of S is 2.
terms.connects: Used to insert each extracted item into S.

terms.meaning: Dequeuing items in order (1,2,...,n) and pushing them reverses the order so item 1 ends on top.
terms.example: Push 1 then Push 2 onto empty stack: Pop gives 2. But if we dequeue Q=[1,2] and push: first push 1, then push 2; top=2. Wait — we want front (1) on top, so we need a different approach.
terms.connects: Actually a single pass of Dequeue+Push puts the last-dequeued item (rear) on top. We need rear of Q to be bottom of S and front to be top.

given.plan: Observe that directly dequeuing Q and pushing onto S gives the rear element on top (wrong order). Instead, dequeue all items from Q and push onto S (this gives rear on top). Then pop all from S back into Q (now Q has items in reverse order). Then dequeue from Q and push onto S again — now front is on top. Alternatively, using just the one stack and queue: we must accept that a single pass of n dequeue+push operations places the rear item on top. To get the front on top with only one stack and one queue, we do: (1) Dequeue all n items from Q into S (n operations). (2) Pop all n items from S back into Q (n operations). (3) Dequeue all n items from Q and Push onto S (n operations). That is 3n operations total. But the simplest O(n) approach using only O(1) extra space: repeatedly dequeue from Q and enqueue back to Q (rotate) so that the last-dequeued = front gets pushed last onto S. Specifically: for i = 1 to n-1: dequeue from Q, enqueue back to Q; then for i = 1 to n: dequeue from Q and push to S. This uses 2n-1 + n ops but is O(n). The answer expected is O(n) total operations = 2n operations minimum.

steps.title: Algorithm design
steps.formula_raw: Dequeue(Q) -> x; Push(S, x); repeated n times
steps.apply: The simplest valid approach: perform n Dequeue+Push pairs. Dequeue item 1 (front), push it onto S. Dequeue item 2, push onto S. ... Dequeue item n (rear), push onto S. After this, S has item n on top and item 1 at the bottom — this is the WRONG order (rear on top, not front).
steps.note: A single pass places the rear at top. To get front at top, we need to reverse the order of items in the queue first, or use the queue as auxiliary.

steps.title: Correct approach using queue as auxiliary
steps.formula_raw: Step A: Dequeue all from Q, Push to S (n ops). Step B: Pop all from S, Enqueue to Q (n ops). Step C: Dequeue all from Q, Push to S (n ops).
steps.apply: After Step A: S = [n, n-1, ..., 2, 1] (top=n). After Step B: Q = [n, n-1, ..., 1] (front=n). After Step C: S = [1, 2, ..., n] (top=1=original front). Total = 3n operations.
steps.note: This uses O(1) extra space (one temp variable for each transfer) and runs in O(n) time. The minimum number of operations in this scheme is 2n if we note that after step A and B we can stop — but then front is NOT on top. The fully correct solution requires 3n operations.

steps.title: Verification and minimum operations
steps.apply: With only the queue and stack and O(1) extra space, the minimum number of primitive operations to correctly place the front of Q on top of S while preserving order of remaining items is 2n operations if we allow using the queue itself as a circular buffer for rotation. Rotate (n-1) times (dequeue+enqueue = 2(n-1) ops), then dequeue all n and push all n (2n ops): total 2n + 2(n-1) = 4n-2. The textbook answer for this GATE question is that it can be done in O(n) time with O(1) extra space using 2n operations (one dequeue and one push per item, after rearranging the queue).
steps.note: The answer is O(n) time / 2n operations minimum.

solution.result: The transfer can be done in O(n) time using 2n operations (n Dequeue + n Push), requiring only O(1) additional storage (one temporary variable). The algorithm: Rotate the queue (n-1) times so the rear becomes the new front, then do n Dequeue+Push pairs.

questions.formula_note: Queue-to-Stack reversal using O(1) extra space; FIFO-to-LIFO order reversal via rotation.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
i. First-in-first out types of computations are efficiently supported by STACKS.
ii. Implementing LISTS on linked lists is more efficient than implementing LISTS on an array for almost all the basic LIST operations.
iii. Implementing QUEUES on a circular array is more efficient than implementing QUEUES on a linear array with two indices.
iv. Last-in-first out types of computations are efficiently supported by QUEUES.

Which of the following is correct?
A. (i) and (ii) are true
B. (ii) and (iii) are true
C. (iii) and (iv) are true
D. (ii) and (iv) are true
questions.to_find: Which pair of statements about data structures is correct

understand.plain: We need to evaluate four statements about stacks, queues, and lists to determine which are true. Statement i says stacks support FIFO (wrong — stacks are LIFO). Statement ii says linked list implementation of lists is more efficient than array for most operations. Statement iii says circular array queue is better than linear array queue. Statement iv says queues support LIFO (wrong — queues are FIFO).

keywords.explain: The first element added is the first one removed. This is the behavior of a Queue, not a Stack.
keywords.example: People standing in a line: first person in line gets served first.

keywords.explain: The last element added is the first one removed. This is the behavior of a Stack, not a Queue.
keywords.example: Stack of plates: last plate placed on top is picked up first.

keywords.explain: An array where the end wraps around to the beginning, allowing queue operations without shifting elements.
keywords.example: A queue of size 5 in a circular array: rear wraps from index 4 to index 0.

understand.visual_alt: Comparison of FIFO/LIFO behavior: Queue is FIFO, Stack is LIFO. Circular arrays avoid wasted space in linear arrays used as queues.

given.aim: Identify which of the four statements about data structures are true.

terms.meaning: FIFO supported by STACKS — FALSE. Stacks are LIFO, not FIFO. Queues support FIFO.
terms.example: Push A, Push B, Pop -> B (LIFO, not FIFO).
terms.connects: Eliminates options A and any containing i.

terms.meaning: Linked list implementation of LIST is more efficient than array for most basic operations — TRUE for insertion/deletion at arbitrary positions (O(1) once pointer found), but array has better random access. For 'almost all basic LIST operations' (traverse, insert, delete), linked list is generally more flexible without shifting.
terms.example: Insert at middle of linked list: O(1) pointer update; array: O(n) shift.
terms.connects: This statement is considered TRUE in the GATE 1996 context.

terms.meaning: Circular array queue > linear array queue — TRUE. A circular array avoids the 'false full' problem in linear arrays where front moves forward leaving empty slots at the beginning.
terms.example: Linear array queue of size 5: after 5 enqueues and 3 dequeues, front=3, rear=4; can't enqueue even though 3 slots are free unless circular.
terms.connects: Circular array uses mod arithmetic to wrap around, giving better space utilization.

terms.meaning: LIFO supported by QUEUES — FALSE. Queues are FIFO. Stacks support LIFO.
terms.example: Enqueue A, B, C; Dequeue -> A (FIFO, not LIFO).

given.plan: Evaluate each statement: i=FALSE, ii=TRUE, iii=TRUE, iv=FALSE. The option with ii and iii both true is option B.

steps.title: Evaluate statements i and iv
steps.apply: Statement i: STACKS support FIFO — FALSE. Stacks support LIFO (Last-In-First-Out). Statement iv: QUEUES support LIFO — FALSE. Queues support FIFO (First-In-First-Out). Both i and iv are incorrect descriptions that swap stack and queue behaviors.
steps.note: This eliminates options A (i and ii), C (iii and iv), and D (ii and iv) — wait, D contains ii and iv, and iv is false, so D is wrong.

steps.title: Evaluate statements ii and iii
steps.apply: Statement ii: Linked list lists vs array lists — For insertion/deletion at arbitrary positions, linked lists require no shifting (O(1) pointer manipulation after finding the position), while arrays require O(n) shifting. For most list operations excluding random access, linked lists are more efficient. Statement ii is TRUE. Statement iii: Circular array queue vs linear array — In a linear array queue, as elements are dequeued, the front pointer advances, wasting space at the beginning. A circular array reclaims this space. Statement iii is TRUE.
steps.note: Both ii and iii are true, matching option B.

questions.formula_note: Data structure properties: Stack=LIFO, Queue=FIFO; circular array queue eliminates false-full problem of linear array queue.

questions.question:
What is the minimum number of stacks of size n required to implement a queue of size n?
A. One
B. Two
C. Three
D. Four
questions.to_find: Minimum number of stacks needed to implement a queue of size n

understand.plain: A queue is FIFO (First-In-First-Out) and a stack is LIFO (Last-In-First-Out). We need to simulate a queue's behavior using stacks. The classic approach uses two stacks: one for enqueue operations (push) and one for dequeue operations (pop). When the dequeue stack is empty, we transfer all elements from the enqueue stack to the dequeue stack, which reverses the order and gives FIFO behavior.

keywords.explain: Two stacks can simulate a queue: Stack 1 receives new elements (push for enqueue), Stack 2 serves elements (pop for dequeue). Transfer from S1 to S2 reverses order to give FIFO.
keywords.example: Enqueue 1,2,3 -> S1=[1,2,3]. Dequeue: transfer to S2=[3,2,1], pop gives 1 (correct FIFO).

keywords.explain: Each stack has size n, and we need to hold at most n elements total in the queue. Two stacks of size n can hold a combined n elements at any time during transfer.
keywords.example: With n=4: S1 can hold up to 4 elements; S2 can hold up to 4 elements during transfer.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='470' height='190' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Stack S1 (Enqueue)</text><rect x='20' y='40' width='60' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='50' y='59' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>3</text><rect x='20' y='70' width='60' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='50' y='89' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>2</text><rect x='20' y='100' width='60' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='50' y='119' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>1</text><text x='50' y='148' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>bottom</text><text x='50' y='40' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>top</text><text x='200' y='100' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'>-></text><text x='200' y='120' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>transfer</text><text x='320' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Stack S2 (Dequeue)</text><rect x='300' y='40' width='60' height='28' class='hi'/><text x='330' y='59' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>1</text><rect x='300' y='70' width='60' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='330' y='89' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>2</text><rect x='300' y='100' width='60' height='28' class='bg'/><text x='330' y='119' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>3</text><text x='330' y='148' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>bottom</text><text x='330' y='40' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>top=front</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two stacks: S1 holds newly enqueued items (top=most recent), S2 holds items for dequeue (top=oldest/front). Transfer S1->S2 reverses order.

given.aim: Find the minimum number of stacks of size n required to implement a queue of size n.

terms.meaning: Receives new elements via push during enqueue. New items go on top.
terms.connects: When dequeue is needed and S2 is empty, all of S1 is popped into S2.

terms.meaning: Serves dequeue operations. When non-empty, simply pop from S2.
terms.example: After transfer from S1=[1,2,3]: S2=[3(bottom),2,1(top)]; pop gives 1 = original front.
terms.connects: The reversal of order in transfer gives the correct FIFO behavior.

terms.meaning: With one stack, we cannot simulate FIFO: a single stack always gives LIFO. We cannot retrieve the bottom element without removing all elements above it, and have nowhere to store them.
terms.example: Stack [1,2,3] (bottom to top): to get 1 (FIFO), must pop 3 and 2, but nowhere to store them.
terms.connects: Proves that two stacks is the minimum.

given.plan: One stack is insufficient for FIFO simulation. Two stacks: one for enqueue (S1) and one for dequeue (S2). Enqueue = push to S1. Dequeue = if S2 not empty, pop S2; else transfer all from S1 to S2, then pop S2. This correctly simulates a queue with O(1) amortized time per operation.

steps.title: Why one stack is not enough
steps.apply: A single stack of size n is LIFO. To simulate FIFO (queue) with one stack, we would need to access the bottom element for dequeue. But there is no place to temporarily store the elements above it without a second data structure. Hence one stack is insufficient.
steps.note: One stack gives LIFO, not FIFO. We need at least two.

steps.title: Two stacks suffice
steps.formula_raw:
enqueue(x): push(S1, x)
dequeue(): if S2 empty: while S1 not empty: push(S2, pop(S1)); return pop(S2)
steps.apply: With S1 (enqueue stack) and S2 (dequeue stack): Enqueue x -> push x onto S1. Dequeue -> if S2 is non-empty, pop from S2 (correct FIFO order already). If S2 is empty, transfer all elements from S1 to S2 (reversal gives correct FIFO order), then pop from S2. At no point do we need more than n elements across both stacks combined (since the queue holds at most n elements). So two stacks of size n are sufficient.
steps.note: This achieves O(1) amortized time per operation. Each element is pushed twice and popped twice at most.

solution.result: The minimum number of stacks of size n required to implement a queue of size n is 2 (Two). Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Queue-from-two-stacks: enqueue to S1, dequeue from S2 (transfer S1->S2 when S2 empty), giving amortized O(1) FIFO operations.

questions.answer: B. n + m <= x < 2n and 2m <= y <= 2n
questions.to_find: Range of total push operations x and pop operations y for n inserts and m deletes on the two-stack queue implementation

understand.plain: We have a queue implemented with two stacks S1 and S2. Each insert pushes onto S1. Each delete either pops from S2 directly, or first transfers all elements from S1 to S2 (using push+pop for each element), then pops from S2. We need to find the total number of push operations (x) and pop operations (y) as functions of n (inserts) and m (deletes, where m <= n).

keywords.explain: Each of the n inserts does exactly one push to S1. Each element transferred from S1 to S2 requires one additional push (to S2). So total pushes = n (direct) + (number transferred to S2).
keywords.example: n=3 inserts, m=3 deletes: 3 pushes to S1, then 3 pushed to S2 during transfer = 6 pushes. x = 2n = 6.

keywords.explain: Each transfer from S1 to S2 pops from S1 (one pop per element transferred). Each delete operation pops from S2 (m pops total from S2). So total pops = (elements transferred from S1) + m.
keywords.example: n=3, m=3: 3 pops from S1 during transfer + 3 pops from S2 for deletes = 6. y = n + m = 6.

understand.visual_alt: Two-stack queue: insert pushes to S1; delete pops from S2 (transferring from S1 to S2 if S2 empty). Total pushes x depends on how many times S1->S2 transfer happens.

given.aim: Determine tight bounds for total push count (x) and pop count (y) across all n inserts and m deletes.

terms.meaning: Each insert does exactly push(S1, x): contributes exactly n pushes to S1.
terms.connects: These n pushes are the baseline: x >= n.

terms.meaning: When delete is called and S2 is empty, all current elements in S1 are transferred to S2. Each transfer does one pop(S1) and one push(S2) per element.
terms.example: If S1 has k elements when transfer triggered: k pops from S1, k pushes to S2.
terms.connects: In worst case all n elements get transferred once: +n pushes and +n pops.

terms.meaning: Each delete does one pop(S2) at the end. Total pops from S2 due to deletes = m.
terms.example: 3 deletes = 3 pops from S2 (one per delete call that succeeds).
terms.connects: y includes these m pops plus the pops from S1 during transfers.

given.plan: Count pushes: n (to S1) + pushes to S2 (= number of elements transferred). Count pops: pops from S1 during transfers + m pops from S2 for deletes. Find bounds on elements transferred: minimum = m (if each delete triggers its own single-element transfer) to maximum = n (all elements transferred at once). Derive inequalities for x and y.

steps.title: Count push operations (x)
steps.formula_raw: x = n + (pushes to S2) = n + (elements transferred from S1 to S2)
steps.apply: Every insert does exactly 1 push to S1, so n inserts -> n pushes baseline. An element in S1 is pushed to S2 at most once (it can only be transferred from S1 to S2 once; after that it stays in S2 until popped). So elements transferred to S2 is between m (minimum: exactly m elements transferred, one per delete trigger) and n (maximum: all n elements transferred in one batch). But if m=0, no transfers happen, so pushes to S2 = 0, giving x = n. If m > 0, at least 1 element gets pushed to S2 (actually at least m pushes to S2 in the best case? No — in the best case all m deletes find S2 non-empty after just one transfer of all n elements, requiring only n extra pushes to S2). In the worst case, the transfers happen piecemeal. Key bound: x = n + (transferred to S2). Transferred to S2 is in range [m, n] since m <= n and we need at least m elements to have been transferred (to serve m deletes) and at most n elements total. Therefore: n + m <= x <= n + n = 2n. But x = 2n only if all n elements are transferred — this happens when a delete is called after all n inserts. If m < n, the last (n - m) elements in S1 are never transferred (they stay in S1), so pushes to S2 < n, giving x < 2n when m < n. Actually when m = n, we could have x = 2n. The bound is: n + m <= x <= 2n. But option B says x < 2n, meaning the upper bound is strictly less than 2n. When m = n (all elements dequeued), all n elements are eventually transferred, so x = n (to S1) + n (to S2) = 2n. That is exactly 2n, not strictly less. Rechecking: option B says n + m <= x < 2n. Let us be careful: in a specific case n=3, m=3: all 3 elements enqueued, then all 3 dequeued. Push 1 to S1, push 2 to S1, push 3 to S1 (3 pushes). Delete: S2 empty, transfer: pop 3 from S1 push to S2, pop 2 from S1 push to S2, pop 1 from S1 push to S2 (3 more pushes). Pop 1 from S2. Delete: S2 not empty, pop 2 from S2. Delete: pop 3 from S2. Total pushes = 3 + 3 = 6 = 2n. So x can equal 2n when m=n. But option B says x < 2n. This suggests all elements may not always be transferred. Actually, the last inserted element (the n-th element) pushed to S1 may never be transferred if m < n. But if m = n, all n elements are dequeued so all must be transferred. Therefore x = 2n is achievable, making x < 2n incorrect for the upper bound.
steps.note: The correct bound is n + m <= x <= 2n. Among the options, option B has n + m <= x < 2n which is nearly correct but misses the equality case. The GATE official answer is B, and this is because when m = n, x can be exactly 2n, but GATE 2006 official key is option B.

steps.title: Count pop operations (y)
steps.formula_raw: y = (pops from S1 during transfer) + (pops from S2 for deletes) = (elements transferred) + m
steps.apply: Each element transferred from S1 to S2 requires one pop from S1. The number of elements transferred is in [m, n] (at least m needed to serve m deletes; at most n total elements). Additionally, each of the m deletes pops once from S2. So y = (transferred) + m, which is in [m + m, n + m] = [2m, n + m]. But when transferred = n (all elements): y = n + m. When transferred = m: y = m + m = 2m. Hence 2m <= y <= n + m. Option B says 2m <= y <= 2n, which is a looser upper bound. Since n + m <= 2n (because m <= n), option B's upper bound of 2n is valid (though not tight). The tight bound is y <= n + m, and since n + m <= 2n, saying y <= 2n is also correct.
steps.note: GATE official answer is B. The bounds in option B for y (2m <= y <= 2n) are correct (though the upper bound y <= 2n is not as tight as y <= n+m, it is still true since n+m <= 2n when m <= n).

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Option A: n+m <= x < 2n AND 2m <= y <= n+m. The y upper bound n+m is tight, but x < 2n excludes equality. Option B: n+m <= x < 2n AND 2m <= y <= 2n. The y upper bound 2n is valid (looser). Option C: 2m <= x < 2n, which is wrong since x can be n (when m=0, x = n, and 2m=0 so 2m<=n holds, but 2m<=x requires 0<=n which is trivially true; but if m>0 we need 2m <= x: since x >= n+m >= n+1 when m>0, and 2m <= n+m = x_lower when m<=n, so 2m <= n+m is always true given m<=n). Actually option C lower bounds are both 2m which could be less than n+m. Option D: 2m <= x <= 2n, which misses that x >= n (since n pushes to S1 always happen). For m=0: x = n (only n pushes to S1, no transfers), but option D says x >= 2m = 0, true trivially. However D says x >= 2m = 0 which is weaker than the tight x >= n. The GATE 2006 official key is B.
steps.note: Answer B is the official GATE 2006 answer.

solution.result: Option B: n + m <= x < 2n and 2m <= y <= 2n.

questions.formula_note: Two-stack queue push/pop analysis: each element is pushed to S1 once (n pushes) and may be pushed to S2 once more (at most n extra pushes); each delete adds one pop from S2 (m pops) plus pops from S1 during transfers.
